full_response:
We adopt the usual “high–energy” natural-unit convention  

  ℏ = c = kB = 1 , ε0 = 1/(4π) ,  

so that  

• energy, mass, inverse time and inverse length all carry the same dimension (energy);  
• a power of “GeV” is therefore sufficient to specify any quantity once the correct power is known.

Useful conversion factors  
(quoted to 4–5 significant figures, more than adequate for the present problem):

1 GeV = 1.602 176 634 × 10⁻¹⁰ J  
1 GeV⁻¹ = 1.973 27 × 10⁻¹⁶ m    (= 0.197 327 fm)  
1 GeV⁻¹ = 6.582 12 × 10⁻²⁵ s  

From these we also have  

1 kg = (c²)⁻¹ × (1 kg) = 5.609 59 × 10²⁶ GeV  
1 m = 5.067 73 × 10¹⁵ GeV⁻¹    (⇒ 1 m⁻³ = 7.683 0 × 10⁻⁴⁸ GeV³)  
1 s = 1.519 27 × 10²⁴ GeV⁻¹   (⇒ 1 s⁻² = 4.328 0 × 10⁻⁴⁹ GeV²)

--------------------------------------------------------------------
1.  Energy: 5 J  

E = 5 J × (1 GeV / 1.602 × 10⁻¹⁰ J)  
  = 3.12 × 10¹⁰ GeV.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
2.  Mass / energy density: ρ = 10 kg m⁻³  

Step 1 – convert kg → GeV:  
10 kg = 10 × 5.609 59 × 10²⁶ GeV = 5.609 6 × 10²⁷ GeV.

Step 2 – convert m⁻³ → GeV³:  
1 m⁻³ = (5.067 73 × 10¹⁵ GeV⁻¹)⁻³ = 7.683 0 × 10⁻⁴⁸ GeV³.

Combine:  
ρ = (5.609 6 × 10²⁷ GeV) × (7.683 0 × 10⁻⁴⁸ GeV³)  
  = 4.31 × 10⁻²⁰ GeV⁴.

(The result carries GeV⁴ because energy-density has mass-dimension four in natural units.)

--------------------------------------------------------------------
3.  Acceleration: a = 3 m s⁻²  

Write a = (3 m)/(1 s)².

Numerator: 3 m = 3 × 5.067 73 × 10¹⁵ GeV⁻¹ = 1.520 × 10¹⁶ GeV⁻¹.  
Denominator: 1 s² = (1.519 27 × 10²⁴ GeV⁻¹)² = 2.309 × 10⁴⁸ GeV⁻².

Therefore  
a = (1.520 × 10¹⁶ GeV⁻¹) / (2.309 × 10⁴⁸ GeV⁻²)  
  = 6.59 × 10⁻³³ GeV.

(With ℏ = c = 1 an acceleration has the dimensions of energy.)

--------------------------------------------------------------------
\boxed{\;5\ \text{J}\;=\;3.1\times10^{10}\ \text{GeV},\quad
       10\ \text{kg/m}^3\;=\;4.3\times10^{-20}\ \text{GeV}^4,\quad
       3\ \text{m/s}^2\;=\;6.6\times10^{-33}\ \text{GeV}\;}

Confidence: 79%
